attributes.stem:
A two-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with fever and respiratory distress. He has had three episodes of pneumonia and two episodes of otitis media in the past year. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His parent mentions that his maternal uncle died in early childhood from a severe infection.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 140/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 45/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and temperature 38.9°C. He has enlarged tonsils and cervical lymph nodes. Chest examination reveals bilateral crackles.

Investigations reveal:

  • Immunoglobulin G: 0.2 g/L (4.0-10.0)

  • Immunoglobulin A: 0.1 g/L (0.2-1.0)

  • Immunoglobulin M: 4.8 g/L (0.4-2.0)

  • Immunoglobulin E: undetectable
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with recurrent bacterial infections and has a distinctive pattern of immunoglobulins that strongly suggests X-linked hyper-IgM syndrome. The key features are elevated IgM with very low or absent levels of other immunoglobulin classes (IgG, IgA, and IgE). The X-linked inheritance pattern is suggested by the history of a maternal uncle dying from severe infection in early childhood.

Common variable immunodeficiency typically presents later in childhood or early adulthood and is characterised by low levels of all immunoglobulins, including IgM. It would not explain the elevated IgM seen in this case.

X-linked agammaglobulinaemia presents with low levels of all immunoglobulin classes and absent B cells. The elevated IgM in this case makes this diagnosis unlikely.

Selective IgA deficiency is characterised by an isolated deficiency of IgA with normal levels of other immunoglobulin classes. It often presents with milder symptoms and would not explain the pattern seen here.

Severe combined immunodeficiency affects both B and T cell function and typically presents with more severe infections earlier in life. While it can affect immunoglobulin levels, it would not typically present with elevated IgM.

X-linked hyper-IgM syndrome is caused by mutations in the CD40 ligand gene, which prevents normal B cell class switching from IgM to other antibody classes. This results in normal or elevated IgM levels but low or absent levels of other antibody classes. These patients are also susceptible to opportunistic infections, particularly Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, due to defective T cell function.
learningPoints: X-linked hyper-IgM syndrome is characterised by recurrent bacterial infections, normal/elevated IgM, and low/absent IgG, IgA, and IgE.
CD40 ligand deficiency on T cells prevents B cell class switching, leading to the characteristic immunoglobulin pattern.
Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis is essential in hyper-IgM syndrome due to defective T cell function.
references: McCusker C, Upton J, Warrington R. Primary immunodeficiency. Allergy Asthma Clin Immunol. 2018;14(Suppl 2):61. Published 2018 Sep 12. doi:10.1186/s13223-018-0290-5
Chinn IK, Orange JS. Immunodeficiency Disorders. Pediatr Rev. 2019;40(5):229-242. doi:10.1542/pir.2017-0308
Lee AY, Gray PE. Evaluating for immunodeficiency in children with recurrent infection. Aust Fam Physician. 2014;43(9):629-632
